figures.caption: TABLE I: Performance comparison on benchmark graph classification datasets. Results are reported as mean ± standard deviation.Bold denotes the best performance, and underline represents the second best performance. * indicates results that we reproduced.

TABLE III: Comparison with the existing methods for transfer learning. Results are reported as mean ± standard deviation. Bold denotes the best performance, and underline represents the second best performance. * indicates results that we reproduced.
Fig. 2: Ablation study comparing CHCL with three ablated variants that remove different components of the proposed framework.
Fig. 4: Performance comparison of CHCL and baseline methods under varying levels of edge dropping and feature masking perturbations.
Fig. 3: Sensitivity analysis of key hyperparameters in CHCL.

Fig. 1: Schematic of CHCL and its capabilities on graph representation learning tasks. CHCL theory embeds Cheeger and Hodge structural priors into contrastive learning to reduce local bias, improve robustness to augmentation perturbations, and enhance structural awareness in learned representations. The framework combines a Cheeger–Hodge encoder with a geometric encoder, and jointly learns local geometric patterns and global structural properties through contrastive objectives. In this paper, CHCL is comprehensively evaluated across multiple datasets and downstream tasks, with additional analyses demonstrating its transferability and structural interpretability.
Fig. 5: Interpretability of the structural semantics learned by CHCL.
